findings.title: CIMA AML Survey for SIBA Registered Persons: submission deadline July 31, 2026 — 11 days away
findings.summary: CIMA's 2026 annual AML Return for Securities Investment Business Act (SIBA) Registered Persons must be submitted by July 31, 2026. The 400+ question return covers AML/CFT policies, procedures, controls, and risk assessments. Non-submission or late submission exposes entities to regulatory action. FATCA/CRS filings also share this July 31 deadline.
findings.client_impact: Any FGC client entity that is a Cayman SIBA Registered Person must complete and file this return by July 31 — 11 days from today. Non-compliant entities risk enforcement action from CIMA, which could jeopardise fund registrations and operational licences.
findings.urgency_reason: Hard regulatory deadline 11 days away. Immediate action is required for any Cayman SIBA Registered Person in FGC's client base.

findings.title: BVI beneficial ownership penalty moratorium expired March 31, 2026: non-compliant entities actively restricted
findings.summary: The BVI FSC's moratorium on late beneficial ownership filing penalties expired March 31, 2026. BVI entities that have not filed UBO information are now marked 'In Penalty' on the FSC system, with operational restrictions affecting corporate transactions, banking relationships, and good standing certificates. Public legitimate-interest access to the register opened April 1, 2026.
findings.client_impact: Any FGC client with a BVI entity that has not completed its beneficial ownership filing is currently unable to conduct corporate transactions or obtain good standing certificates, creating immediate barriers to banking and transactional activity. Each such entity requires urgent remediation.
findings.urgency_reason: The penalty status is active now — clients with non-compliant BVI entities face live operational restrictions that block banking and corporate transactions until the filing is made and the penalty cleared.

findings.title: India integrates FATCA/CRS automatic exchange data into Annual Information Statement from July 8, 2026
findings.summary: India's CBDT has authorised upload of information received under the FATCA/CRS automatic exchange framework into individual taxpayers' Annual Information Statements (AIS) from July 8, 2026. Indian tax authorities will now cross-reference offshore financial account data received from treaty partners against each taxpayer's AIS, dramatically increasing visibility of foreign accounts and assets.
findings.client_impact: Indian-resident clients with offshore accounts, trusts, or SPVs in FATCA/CRS-reporting jurisdictions are now directly visible to the Indian Revenue through the AIS system — discrepancies between declared foreign income and FATCA/CRS data create immediate audit and penalty risk. Clients who have not fully disclosed foreign assets should seek Indian tax counsel without delay.
findings.urgency_reason: This change took effect July 8, 2026 — 12 days ago. The Indian Revenue can now match offshore data to AIS returns immediately. Clients with outstanding disclosure gaps face live audit exposure and should be contacted now.

findings.title: Brazil double-layer taxation on foreign trusts: 15% CFC income tax (Lei 14.754/2023) + up to 8% ITCMD (LC 227/2026) now fully operative
findings.summary: Brazilian residents holding assets through foreign trusts face two layers of Brazilian taxation in 2026: a 15% flat federal income tax on annual trust earnings under Lei 14.754/2023 (regardless of distributions), and state-level ITCMD at progressive rates up to 8% on distributions or transfers under Complementary Law 227/2026. The amnesty period for regularisation of offshore trust assets expired in 2024, making full disclosure mandatory.
findings.client_impact: UHNW Brazilian clients with offshore trusts now face a combined effective rate that can reach 23% or more on trust-level income plus distributions, fundamentally changing the economics of common offshore succession structures. Existing structures should be reviewed for restructuring options or recalibration of distribution timing.
findings.urgency_reason: Both tax layers are now fully operative in 2026; no immediate 30-day deadline, but the cumulative tax burden on trust structures is material and clients should be engaged proactively before year-end Brazilian income tax planning cycles begin.

findings.title: LC 227/2026 mandates progressive ITCMD (up to 8%) on foreign inheritances, gifts, and trust distributions received by Brazilian residents
findings.summary: Complementary Law 227/2026 implements Constitutional Amendment 132/2023, requiring all Brazilian states to adopt progressive ITCMD rates (2%–8%) on assets inherited or gifted from abroad, including foreign trust distributions. Articles 146–151 specifically address trusts with multiple potential ITCMD trigger points: at creation, on annual earnings attribution, and on distribution — creating triple-trigger exposure.
findings.client_impact: Brazilian-resident beneficiaries of foreign trusts now face ITCMD at progressive rates at each possible trigger event, not just on final distribution — meaning clients could incur ITCMD annually on attributed income in addition to federal CFC tax, with a further ITCMD hit at distribution. Trust deed review and state-by-state transition analysis is advisable for all Brazilian clients with foreign trust interests.
findings.urgency_reason: LC 227/2026 is enacted and states must now transition to progressive rates; the triple-trigger exposure is a material change to succession planning assumptions for Brazilian clients. No 30-day deadline, but mid-year review is appropriate given the year the law took effect.

findings.title: Swiss Federal Transparency Register (TJPG) effective October 1, 2026: mandatory UBO filing for Swiss legal entities — 73 days away
findings.summary: Switzerland's Federal Transparency Register enters into force October 1, 2026. Swiss companies, foundations, and certain foreign entities with a Swiss nexus must report ultimate beneficial owners (25%+ threshold) to a centralised electronic register operated by the Federal Office of Justice. The register is non-public. Initial reporting deadlines begin end-2026 to early 2027. Supersedes existing CO/AMLA share-register and beneficial-ownership rules.
findings.client_impact: Clients holding assets through Swiss companies, foundations, or mixed Swiss-nexus structures must identify their UBO chain, prepare documentation, and be ready to file with the Federal Office of Justice by year-end 2026. This is a significant new compliance layer for Swiss-holding wealth structures.
findings.urgency_reason: October 1, 2026 is 73 days away — the law enters force very soon even if initial reporting deadlines extend into early 2027. Clients should begin UBO mapping now to meet year-end filing windows comfortably.

findings.title: Cayman Islands Beneficial Ownership Transparency Act (2026 Revision) published January 29, 2026: enhanced UBO obligations across all registered entities
findings.summary: The Beneficial Ownership Transparency Act (2026 Revision) was published January 29, 2026, consolidating and enhancing UBO information collection and maintenance requirements. It clarifies the beneficial owner definition, strengthens enforcement, and aligns with international transparency standards across all Cayman companies, LLPs, and limited partnerships not exempt under CIMA registration.
findings.client_impact: Clients with Cayman structures should review their beneficial ownership registers against the 2026 Revision's updated definitions and enforcement provisions to ensure current compliance — particularly structures with layered or nominee arrangements that may fall within the clarified beneficial owner definition.
findings.urgency_reason: The 2026 Revision is already in force; no 30-day deadline, but given the strengthened enforcement provisions, a periodic compliance review is prudent for all Cayman-registered client entities.

findings.title: Cayman SIBA Amendment Bill consultation underway: proposed changes to fund manager registration, capital requirements, and supervisory powers
findings.summary: A public consultation on a draft Securities Investment Business (Amendment) Bill, 2026 was launched May 7, 2026. It covers proposed reforms to registration categories, capital requirements, and CIMA supervisory powers affecting registered investment managers, fund administrators, and broker-dealers operating in or from the Cayman Islands.
findings.client_impact: Clients using Cayman-based fund managers or investment management structures should monitor this consultation — the outcome may change registration categories or increase capital and compliance obligations for entities currently operating under existing SIBA exemptions or registrations.
findings.urgency_reason: Consultation is ongoing; no immediate 30-day filing deadline but proposed changes could materially affect Cayman fund structures. Clients and advisers should track the Bill's progress and engage counsel if their structures are in scope.

findings.title: MAS revised Single Family Office (SFO) framework effective June 15, 2026: mandatory notification, AUM thresholds, and annual returns
findings.summary: The MAS SFO framework, effective June 15, 2026, replaces bespoke case-by-case exemptions with a streamlined notification model. SFOs notify MAS, maintain an MAS-licensed bank account, and file annual returns disclosing total AUM and bank name. Minimum AUM thresholds and Singapore-nexus requirements apply. No Capital Markets Services licence is required.
findings.client_impact: UHNW clients operating or considering a Singapore Single Family Office must now comply with the formal notification and annual return obligations under the new framework — existing SFOs operating under previous bespoke arrangements should confirm their status and file if they have not already done so.
findings.urgency_reason: The framework took effect June 15, 2026; existing SFOs should have notified MAS. Those that have not done so are operating without the new formal framework's protections and may be non-compliant. No 30-day deadline today, but remediation should be actioned promptly.

findings.title: FinCEN proposes fundamental AML/CFT program reform: risk-based framework to replace technical compliance model, final rule expected with 12-month implementation period
findings.summary: On April 7, 2026, FinCEN and three prudential regulators issued an NPRM to replace current BSA AML/CFT program requirements with a risk-based framework requiring formal risk assessments and demonstrated program effectiveness. The comment period closed June 9, 2026. A final rule is expected with a 12-month implementation period — the most significant BSA/AML reform in two decades.
findings.client_impact: U.S. financial institutions serving FGC clients — including broker-dealers, RIAs, and banks — will need to restructure AML/CFT compliance programs away from check-box controls toward documented risk assessment and measurable effectiveness, affecting how client due diligence and monitoring are conducted.
findings.urgency_reason: No immediate 30-day deadline, but the comment period has closed and a final rule is now in the pipeline. Clients and advisers with U.S. regulated entities should begin assessing gap against the proposed risk-based framework to get ahead of implementation.

findings.title: SEC 'Regulation Crypto' NPRMs targeted for July 2026: crypto offerings, broker-dealer custody, and market structure
findings.summary: The SEC has targeted July 2026 for three NPRMs under its Regulation Crypto framework: (1) crypto asset offerings and safe harbors for token sales; (2) broker-dealer capital and customer-protection rules for crypto assets; and (3) crypto market structure and exchange regulation. Investment management amendments governing RIA and fund custody of crypto assets are also in preparation.
findings.client_impact: Clients who hold digital assets through U.S.-registered investment advisers or fund vehicles need to monitor these NPRMs — changes to custody rules and safe harbors will directly affect how crypto holdings are structured, held, and reported. Comment periods of 60–90 days will follow publication, creating near-term engagement opportunities.
findings.urgency_reason: NPRMs are expected this month (July 2026), meaning the formal regulatory framework for crypto within U.S. investment management is about to materialise; clients with significant crypto exposure should begin scenario planning now.

findings.title: Panama Agreement 1-2026: UBO disclosure threshold lowered to 10%, digital identity verification mandatory for Superintendency of Banks licensees
findings.summary: Panama's Superintendency of Banks issued Agreement 1-2026, effective March 2026, repealing Agreement 10-2015. Key changes: beneficial ownership disclosure threshold lowered from 25% to 10%; mandatory digital ID verification and inferential geolocation; full chain-to-natural-person documentation required for enhanced CDD. Aligns Panama with FATF Recommendations ahead of anticipated FATF reviews.
findings.client_impact: Clients with Panama banking relationships or Panama-based structures where a shareholder holds 10%–24% of equity must now be disclosed as beneficial owners to Panamanian banks under Agreement 1-2026 — a material expansion that may surface previously non-disclosed ownership chains and trigger enhanced due diligence.
findings.urgency_reason: Agreement 1-2026 is already in force (March 2026). Clients with Panama bank accounts or corporate structures should verify that their holding chain disclosures reflect the new 10% threshold. No 30-day deadline, but ongoing compliance gaps carry regulatory risk.

findings.title: CySEC PS-02-2026 strengthens Cyprus trust UBO register: all trust parties (settlors, trustees, protectors, beneficiaries) must be filed
findings.summary: CySEC Policy Statement PS-02-2026 (published June 1, 2026) requires trustees of Cyprus trusts to file UBO information for all trust parties — settlors, trustees, protectors, beneficiaries, and anyone exercising effective control — with tightened verification. Public access remains suspended (post-ECJ January 2023 ruling); access limited to AML-obliged entities for CDD.
findings.client_impact: Clients who are settlors, beneficiaries, or protectors of Cyprus trusts may now be subject to disclosure obligations they were not previously required to satisfy — trustees need to review current trust registrations against the expanded PS-02-2026 requirements and file any missing UBO data.
findings.urgency_reason: The policy statement is in force as of June 2026 with no specific 30-day deadline today, but trustees of Cyprus trusts who have not updated their UBO filings are in non-compliance with CySEC requirements.

findings.title: Malta Legal Notices 82 and 83 of 2026: updated AML/CFT customer due diligence and expanded Centralised Bank Account Register (effective April 1, 2026)
findings.summary: Legal Notice 82/2026 amends Malta's Prevention of Money Laundering and Funding of Terrorism Regulations, and Legal Notice 83/2026 amends the Centralised Bank Account Register Regulations, both effective April 1, 2026. The amendments update CDD requirements, enhance UBO verification, and expand the bank account register's coverage to improve competent authority access.
findings.client_impact: Clients with Malta-based companies or trusts should confirm that their CDD files held by Malta-regulated intermediaries reflect the updated verification standards — and that their bank account register entries are current — to avoid compliance deficiencies in Malta-supervised structures.
findings.urgency_reason: Both Legal Notices have been in force since April 1, 2026. While there is no 30-day deadline today, clients with Malta structures should verify their intermediaries have updated CDD files in line with the new requirements.

findings.title: Central Bank of the Bahamas Q2 2026: updated AML guidelines in force, CFATF Fifth Round Mutual Evaluation onsite in October 2026
findings.summary: The Central Bank of The Bahamas published its Q2 2026 Quarterly Letter on July 3, 2026, covering updated AML/CFT/CPF Guidelines (effective May 4, 2026) and preparations for the CFATF Fifth Round Mutual Evaluation onsite visit in October 2026. Supervised institutions are expected to demonstrate enhanced risk-based CDD and transaction monitoring.
findings.client_impact: Bahamas-based financial institutions serving FGC clients are likely to increase CDD requirements and documentation demands in the lead-up to the October 2026 CFATF evaluation — clients with Bahamas banking or fund relationships should anticipate heightened KYC requests in the near term.
findings.urgency_reason: CFATF evaluation is 10+ weeks away; Bahamas financial institutions are intensifying compliance ahead of the review. No direct client deadline today, but enhanced CDD requests from Bahamas counterparties are expected imminently.

findings.title: Guernsey Private Trust Companies must now hold full fiduciary licence or 'limited permission' from GFSC
findings.summary: Following GFSC guidance published September 2025, all Guernsey Private Trust Companies (PTCs) must hold either a full fiduciary licence or a GFSC 'limited permission.' The guidance closed a previous gap where some PTCs operated without formal authorisation. PTCs established for family wealth planning must now be GFSC-registered and supervised.
findings.client_impact: UHNW clients using Guernsey PTCs for succession or dynastic planning need to confirm that their PTC holds the appropriate GFSC authorisation — PTCs operating without a licence or limited permission are in breach of the updated guidance and may face enforcement.
findings.urgency_reason: GFSC guidance has been in effect since September 2025 and any Guernsey PTC still operating without proper authorisation is currently non-compliant. Clients should urgently verify their PTC's licensing status.

findings.title: Bahamas Domestic Minimum Top-Up Tax Amendment Bill 2026: Pillar Two refinements targeting July 1, 2026 effective date
findings.summary: The Bahamas DMTT (Amendment) Bill, 2026 introduces administrative amendments to the DMTT regime — the Bahamas' Pillar Two-compliant global minimum tax — targeting a July 1, 2026 effective date. The Bill addresses administrative procedures, penalties, and technical alignment with the OECD January 2026 Side-by-Side package. Applies to constituent entities of MNE groups with global revenue exceeding EUR 750M.
findings.client_impact: Clients who are part of large multinational enterprise groups with Bahamas-based constituent entities and group revenues exceeding EUR 750M should review the amended DMTT compliance requirements with their Pillar Two advisers, particularly changes to penalty provisions and the Side-by-Side safe harbour alignment.
findings.urgency_reason: The July 1, 2026 effective date is now past; clients with Bahamas MNE-group entities at or above the EUR 750M threshold should confirm the amended provisions have been incorporated into their Pillar Two compliance programs.

findings.title: OECD CARF (Crypto-Asset Reporting Framework) live from January 1, 2026: 48-jurisdiction first wave including EU, UK, Canada, Japan, South Korea
findings.summary: CARF went live January 1, 2026 across 48 jurisdictions. RCASPs (exchanges, wallet providers, etc.) in the EU (via DAC8), UK, Canada, Japan, and South Korea must collect user self-certifications and report transaction-level crypto data annually for exchange with partner tax authorities. Singapore and UAE are in the second wave targeting first exchanges in 2028.
findings.client_impact: Clients with crypto holdings on exchanges or via service providers based in CARF-implementing jurisdictions now have their crypto transaction data — including crypto-to-fiat, crypto-to-crypto, transfers, and payments — automatically reportable to their home tax authority, closing the information gap that previously made crypto assets opaque to tax authorities.
findings.urgency_reason: CARF is already operative. Clients who have not yet aligned their tax filings with their actual crypto activity across CARF-reporting exchanges are at risk of first-year notices or audits when CARF data lands at their tax authority. No single 30-day deadline, but 2025 tax returns in many jurisdictions are due or recently filed.

findings.title: OECD Pillar Two May 2026 guidance: late-filing penalty framework, UTPR Safe Harbour extension for 52/53-week fiscal years, GloBE XML schema released
findings.summary: On May 18, 2026, the OECD released transitional relief on late-filing penalties for first-year GloBE Information Return filers, extended the Transitional UTPR Safe Harbour to 52–53-week fiscal year entities, and updated the Central Record of qualifying jurisdictions. The GloBE Information Return XML Schema was released June 2026 for calendar-year taxpayers whose first GIR was due June 30, 2026.
findings.client_impact: Clients in Pillar Two-scope MNE groups (global revenue >EUR 750M) with first GloBE Information Return filing obligations should note the transitional penalty relief and confirm that their GIR filings — due June 30, 2026 for calendar-year groups — were submitted, or determine whether they qualify for relief.
findings.urgency_reason: The first GloBE Information Return was due June 30, 2026 for calendar-year groups — already passed. Clients whose advisers have not yet filed or confirmed relief eligibility should follow up immediately.

findings.title: Netherlands Pillar Two Safe Harbours Bill: consultation closed July 14, 2026 — Side-by-Side package safe harbours not yet available in the Netherlands
findings.summary: The Dutch Ministry of Finance's consultation on the Draft Safe Harbours Bill (implementing the OECD January 2026 Side-by-Side package) closed July 14, 2026. Until the Bill is enacted, the Side-by-Side Safe Harbour (allowing US GILTI/CAMT to coexist with Dutch Pillar Two), UPE Safe Harbour, Substance-based Tax Incentive Safe Harbour, and Simplified ETR Safe Harbour are not available to Dutch entities.
findings.client_impact: Clients with Dutch holding companies or operating entities in Pillar Two-scope MNE groups cannot rely on the OECD Side-by-Side safe harbours for Dutch purposes until the Bill passes — meaning their Dutch Pillar Two exposure may be larger than modelled if structures were planned assuming safe harbour availability.
findings.urgency_reason: Consultation has just closed (July 14, 2026) and the Bill is in the legislative pipeline. Clients with Dutch structures in Pillar Two scope should pressure-test their 2026 Pillar Two positions without the Side-by-Side safe harbour until the Bill is enacted.

findings.title: Luxembourg minimum net wealth tax (NWT) regime amended: optional dividend and capital gains exemption pathway introduced for holding vehicles
findings.summary: Luxembourg tabled amendments to its minimum NWT regime for collective investment vehicles and certain holding companies. Qualifying entities may now elect a tax exemption on certain dividend income and capital gains in lieu of the standard NWT calculation, reducing the NWT burden for Luxembourg SPF, SOPARFI, and similar holding structures that derive primarily passive income.
findings.client_impact: Clients using Luxembourg SPF or SOPARFI structures for international wealth holding may benefit from the new exemption election, potentially reducing annual NWT costs — advisers managing these structures should evaluate whether the election is advantageous on a case-by-case basis.
findings.urgency_reason: Positive change already tabled, no action deadline — background awareness for existing Luxembourg holding structure clients. No immediate planning urgency.

findings.title: Luxembourg 20% personal income tax credit for investments in young innovative companies (YICs) from 2026 tax year
findings.summary: From the 2026 tax year, Luxembourg tax residents may claim a 20% PIT credit on qualifying investments of at least EUR 10,000 in fully paid-up shares of young innovative companies. The credit is non-refundable and must be taken in the year of investment. Similar to France's IR-PME and the UK's EIS/SEIS.
findings.client_impact: Luxembourg-resident HNW clients with appetite for early-stage venture investments can now receive a 20% tax credit on qualifying YIC investments — reducing the effective cost of startup participation as part of a diversified alternative investment strategy.
findings.urgency_reason: Informational — the credit is available from the 2026 tax year. No immediate deadline; relevant when advising Luxembourg-resident clients on alternative investment allocations.

findings.title: FINMA Circular 2026/1: climate and nature-related financial risk governance mandatory for FINMA-supervised banks, insurers, and asset managers from January 1, 2026
findings.summary: FINMA Circular 2026/1 requires all FINMA-supervised entities — including Swiss private banks and wealth managers — to integrate climate-related financial risks into governance, risk management, and disclosures. Phase 1 covers climate risks (2026); broader nature-related risk scope follows in a second phase.
findings.client_impact: Swiss private banks and FINMA-supervised wealth managers serving FGC clients now have mandatory climate risk governance obligations, which may affect investment mandates, asset eligibility criteria, and disclosure requirements for client portfolios managed out of Switzerland.
findings.urgency_reason: Already in force since January 1, 2026. Background awareness for clients with Swiss-managed assets — the impact flows through the bank or asset manager rather than requiring direct client action.

findings.title: ADGM FSRA proposes lighter licensing and capital regime for smaller fund managers (under USD 200M) and institutional-only managers
findings.summary: The ADGM FSRA issued a consultation proposing reduced minimum capital requirements, streamlined licensing, and compliance exemptions for ADGM-based private fund managers with committed capital up to USD 200M or managers whose funds target solely large institutional investors. Designed to attract boutique fund managers and family office fund structures to ADGM.
findings.client_impact: UHNW families considering establishing a regulated fund management entity in the UAE may find ADGM increasingly attractive once this proposal is enacted — lower capital and licensing thresholds would reduce set-up costs for family office fund structures and boutique investment managers.
findings.urgency_reason: This is a consultation proposal — no enacted rules yet, and no client action required today. Background awareness for families exploring UAE-regulated fund establishment.
